body: After months of searching (and also putting it off cause procrastination) I finally booked an appointment with an in-network psychologist to be evaluated. I'm a little bummed that it's in February, but I still managed to find something finally. I feel like I'm actually getting somewhere with finding out if I actually have it or not.
response: That's great to hear that you've taken a significant step in booking an appointment with a psychologist, even if it feels like it took a while to get there. It's completely normal to experience some procrastination when it comes to addressing mental health concerns since it can be both overwhelming and daunting. Although February might seem a bit far off, it's encouraging that you've secured a spot, and this gives you plenty of time to prepare for your evaluation. Consider using this time to jot down any symptoms, concerns, or questions you might have regarding your mental health—this can help you feel more prepared for your appointment. It's also a good opportunity to reflect on how certain aspects of your daily life are affected. Remember that seeking professional help is a courageous step towards better understanding yourself and your needs. In the meantime, try to cultivate a positive routine that supports your mental health, whether through exercise, meditation, or creative outlets. You’re on a promising path, and just by setting up this appointment, you've already made meaningful progress. Keep moving forward with optimism; every small step is still a step in the right direction.

body: Recently put on this drug at 30mg daily, on day 7 and the side effects are all I’m getting from this, ibs, severe crushing headache all day long, squinting the whole day because my sinuses, eyes and head hurt. Stomach is suffering bad plus permanent nausea all day. No positives at all yet, no focus just adding to the problems I have with nothing more than side effects. Does this change?? Does it get better because I had high hopes and now I’m on them it’s just awful to the point I’m debating just stopping entirely already. I wish I knew if any of this would improve.
response: It sounds like you're experiencing a tough time with the new medication, and I can imagine how frustrating and discouraging that must be. The symptoms you're describing, such as gastrointestinal issues, headaches, and nausea, are not uncommon during the initial phase of starting a new medication. Often, the body takes time to adjust, and some side effects may diminish over the course of a few weeks. However, it's essential to listen to your body and consider how severe the side effects are. If they are significantly impacting your quality of life, this is crucial information to discuss with your healthcare provider. They might suggest adjusting the dosage, switching to a different medication, or implementing strategies to alleviate some of the side effects. It's also important to consider that while some people eventually notice an improvement, others may not experience the desired benefits from a specific medication. Your doctor can guide you on whether it's worth continuing in hopes of improvement or if alternatives should be considered. Always make any changes to your medication regimen under the guidance of your healthcare provider to ensure your safety and well-being.

body: Hi. I'm fairly new to stimulant use, and I'm hoping you can help me understand what's going on. I started with Adderall. It was pretty good, but 20 mg didn't do that much and 25 or 30mg caused fast heart rate, irritability when wearing off, insomnia. Tried 30 mg XR - still insomnia.  Moved to Zenzedi and it was great, but it's been out of stock and I had to get generic this month. I've been more moody and now have mild depression. I called the pharmacist and he said the generic shouldn't be doing that. I'm wondering if maybe now that I've been on Dextroamphetamine for two months now if maybe it's a dose issue?? Do you think these side effects could be from too low or too high a dose? It's something I'm wondering about.
response:
Navigating stimulant medication adjustments can be quite complex, and it's not uncommon to experience fluctuations in how you feel as you alter dosages or switch formulations. The side effects you're experiencing, like a fast heart rate, irritability, insomnia, and mood changes, can indeed result from both too high and too low a dose. A higher dose may enhance those stimulatory effects, leading to increased heart rate and agitation, especially as the medication wears off. Conversely, if the dose is too low, you might not gain the therapeutic benefits you're seeking, which could possibly lead to feelings of low mood or mild depression if your original symptoms are not being adequately managed.

It's worth noting that generic medications can sometimes cause differences in how one feels due to slight variations in their formulation, despite having the same active ingredient. Although generics are considered bioequivalent to their brand-name counterparts, some individuals are sensitive to even small changes. Given that you've experienced success with Zenzedi, it might be the case that your body responds better to that particular formulation compared to the generic. Additionally, your mood changes could be an adjustment to being on the medication over a period of time, or your body adapting to the stimulants, which might also influence your overall tolerance and response.

Consider discussing with your healthcare provider about these side effects. They might recommend bloodwork, altering the dose, or even trying a different medication or formulation to better suit your needs. It's crucial to monitor these symptoms closely and work closely with your healthcare provider to find the right balance. Adjustments to stimulant medications often require a bit of trial and error, and it's important to communicate how each change affects you. Additionally, make sure to incorporate healthy practices like maintaining a regular sleep schedule, staying hydrated, and managing stress levels, which can all play a role in how these medications affect you.

body: I'm wondering how I can deal with those moments where I suddenly feel drained and just want to sleep. Medication has definitely helped me, these moments are less common, but I've had to go a couple days without any and just can't seem to do anything but nap. It's so frustrating because I want to paint, go outside etc, but I just can't. Everything is foggy and too overwhelming and exhausting.
response: It sounds like you're having a tough time managing the occasional resurgence of fatigue and it’s understandable that this can be quite frustrating, especially when you want to engage in activities you enjoy. When your medication isn’t available and you’re feeling drained, it might be helpful to focus on a gentle routine that doesn’t require too much energy. Consider trying some deep breathing exercises or a bit of light stretching to gently rejuvenate your energy levels. Sometimes, focusing on the smallest tasks – like making a cup of tea or sitting by a window with some natural light – can slowly shift your mindset. It might also help to break down your desired activities into smaller, more manageable steps. For example, if painting feels too overwhelming, maybe start by setting up your paints and taking a few minutes to think about what inspires you today. Don’t hesitate to reach out to loved ones or friends to talk about what you're experiencing. Sometimes just sharing how you’re feeling can lighten the burden a bit. Also, keep in mind that it's okay to rest and listen to your body's needs. Practicing self-compassion and patience with yourself will help create a nurturing space to regain your energy.

body: So, since i was diagnosed with ocd, my libido went from mega high to almost non existant. My psych prescribed me some buproprion, but last month my libido was pretty normal, only on this month my libido lower3d (not like the 1st month, but is low) i also read that the buproprion could boost libido on some users, should i really take the buproprion? 20 years old, male
response:
Deciding whether to take bupropion to address changes in your libido involves considering your specific circumstances and consulting with your healthcare provider. You've mentioned that your libido fluctuated after your OCD diagnosis and is currently lower than usual, which is not uncommon as both OCD and medications used to treat it can impact sexual desire. It's worth noting that bupropion differs from other antidepressants since it is often associated with fewer sexual side effects and may increase libido in some users. However, everyone reacts differently to medications, so it's not guaranteed to have the same effect for you.

It's crucial to discuss these changes and your concerns with your psychiatrist, as they can consider all factors, including the severity of your OCD symptoms, mental and physical health, and overall treatment plan. They may evaluate your current condition, the benefits of bupropion, and any potential risks associated with its use for your specific case. Furthermore, if you've noticed a pattern or trigger that affects your libido, sharing this with your doctor can be helpful in making an informed decision about starting or continuing bupropion. Your age, lifestyle, and personal preferences are also important factors to discuss. Remember, any changes in medication should be made under professional guidance to ensure your well-being and address any possible complications.

body: why do I seem to copy other people's words like when someone says something brave funny revolting I tend to imagine saying the exact same thing to someone in my head i also create a lot of social scenario in my head is this something I should stop doing. I do have ocd .is this something related to ocd
response:
It's not uncommon for people to internalize and mimic the words and expressions of others, especially when they have a more intense emotional impact, such as being brave, funny, or revolting. This tendency can be related to our natural inclination to learn through imitation, a process that helps us navigate social interactions by absorbing the expressions we find effective or admirable. Creating social scenarios in your head is a form of mental rehearsal, allowing you to prepare for various interactions by trying out different responses and outcomes. This can be particularly common in individuals who experience anxiety or OCD, as these scenarios can serve as a way to anticipate and manage potential social stressors.

With OCD, intrusive thoughts and the compulsion to repeatedly rehearse or analyze social interactions can be heightened. Your mind might latch onto specific phrases or scenarios because they provoke strong emotional responses, and rehearsing them can become a way to exert a sense of control or comfort. However, while reflecting on or experimenting with social dialogue in your mind isn't inherently negative, it could become problematic if it starts interfering significantly with your day-to-day life. If you find yourself excessively preoccupied or distressed by these imaginings, seeking professional advice could be beneficial. A mental health professional, particularly one familiar with OCD, can help determine if what you're experiencing is a symptom of the condition and offer strategies to manage it more effectively. They might employ cognitive-behavioral techniques to help you recognize and regulate these thoughts, moving towards more balanced and productive thinking patterns.

body: One of my strategies to calm myself down is to just think in my head and kinda talk to myself to come to terms. But theirs so much stuff going on I can’t even do that anymore without getting distracted with an instrusive thought or a compulsion. I start talking to myself and then switch to something completely different and its just exhausting. I have started new medication that should help with this but holt f*ck it’s exhausting.
response: It sounds incredibly challenging to navigate through such a whirlwind of thoughts and distractions, especially when you're trying to use self-talk as a calming strategy. It’s understandable that with a lot going on in your mind, staying focused becomes difficult and frustrating. Intrusive thoughts and compulsions can definitely add layers of complexity to what should be a soothing practice. It's good to hear that you’re starting medication, as that might provide some relief, though it's important to remember that it may take a bit of time to see significant changes. In the meantime, perhaps incorporating other calming techniques alongside your self-talk might help, such as mindfulness or deep-breathing exercises. These techniques can sometimes create a bit of mental space and make it easier to guide your thoughts back to the conversation you're having with yourself. Remember, it's okay to be patient with yourself during this process, as managing these feelings and thoughts can take time. You might also consider discussing these experiences with your healthcare provider, as they could offer tailored strategies or adjustments to your medication. Engaging with a therapist could also provide you with additional coping mechanisms and a supportive space to work through these challenges. Taking small steps daily to center yourself can gradually lead to a more peaceful mental environment, even when distractions arise.

body: i am so!!! tired of this!!! everyday i have real event ocd and feel sick and GUILTY and want to die!! and i have so many intrusive thoughts about not only my suffering but all the suffering the world. like animal abuse. i can’t even look at cute animals pics or barely even use beauty products anymore because i think of animal cruelty. i am obviously vegan as well but when i go to the store i feel like bawling seeing the animal products. i think about humans who are working in sweatshops and girls who get married off as children and people with severe disabilities and old people and EVERYTHING i’m so tired i think of this stuff all the time. it’s a loop in my head over and over and over. then i have a fear of being poisoned. and a fear of wondering if anything is even real. and then the real event ocd is truly killing me i feel like if i die i’ll go to hell. oh, and that’s another one of my obsessions, being freaked out that a god in the sky could be 24/7 watching me. FUCKKKK:(!!!
response: I'm really sorry to hear that you're going through such a tough time. It sounds incredibly overwhelming to carry the weight of the world on your shoulders, especially with the combination of obsessive thoughts and the constant guilt surrounding things largely out of your control. It's important first to recognize that these thoughts and feelings, while intensely real and distressing, don't define who you are. Seeking professional help can be incredibly beneficial, and a therapist can work with you to develop strategies to manage these intrusive thoughts and feelings of guilt. Remember that being aware and considerate of others' suffering is a testament to your empathy, but it's crucial to set boundaries for your well-being. Taking small breaks from certain triggers, like avoiding distressing media, might give you a little room to breathe. Try to find activities that genuinely bring you a sense of peace, whether that’s through art, nature, or something that simply distracts your mind from heavy thoughts. Connecting with communities or support groups where others may share similar experiences can also provide comfort and understanding. It’s okay to take care of yourself so you’re better equipped to extend care and compassion to others. Life can feel relentless, and thoughts can feel like they're closing in, but you're not alone in this, and there's a path through it with the right support and guidance.

body: 23 years old male and I have never had a friend or romantic relationship. I don’t leave my house nor talk to anybody new just my family because I’m paralyzed in fear of I don’t even know what anymore. My life has been a constant struggle of fighting random mental illness problems like health anxiety as a teen constantly thinking I was having a heart attack from chest pains but then realizing it was just acid reflux from all my anxiety.  My younger years 8-12 were plagued with panic attacks and selective mutism. I was put a special class for grades 3-5 and I had to repeat grade 4 because I missed too many days of school. I was the only person with autism or at that time “anxiety” with a room full of kids with behavioral problems that resorted to violence in a drop of a hat. I basically just didn’t talk or do much of anything for my elementary years because of that environment. I was never bullied mainly I think because I could mask ok when people actually got to talk to me but I never could relax and I never felt like my self. I still feel like that kid in elementary school living in a world that I don’t belong avoiding everything but also longing companionship but unable to do anything about it.
response: It sounds like you're dealing with a complex mixture of anxiety, social challenges, and possibly some unresolved issues from your childhood experiences. Feeling paralyzed by fear and unable to form connections is incredibly tough, especially when past experiences have been so daunting. The environment you described during your school years, being placed in a setting that wasn't fully tailored to your needs, might have contributed significantly to how you perceive social interactions today. Your desire for companionship alongside the fear of stepping out is a common conflict for those who have similar backgrounds. It might help to start with small steps towards interaction—such as joining an online community or participating in activities that pique your interest in a low-pressure environment. Professional guidance can also be very beneficial, as a mental health professional could support you in working through your anxiety and past experiences. They can offer coping strategies for anxiety and help you build the confidence to engage more with the world around you. Remember, it's important to acknowledge and validate your feelings because they're real and significant, but also to recognize there's hope and potential for change and connection.

body: Whenever i face a very stressful confrontation with someone, i can barely think. My mind becomes foggy, recollection of memory is poor, i may misspeak, and i sometimes conflate things when trying to explain the cause of my actions. I don’t know if that’s just extreme anxiety, or if that’s my mindset when I shutdown, or both. There’s times where that reaction and my behavior is interpreted as guilt because i don’t explain it properly, or they don’t understand, even if I’m trying to be completely truthful. I can’t help but wonder if anyone else has ever been in an argument, felt confronted, and just couldn’t handle the stress from it. I know for me, situations like that feel overwhelming, and I’m not always sure how to deal with it.
response:
What you're experiencing is actually quite common and you're certainly not alone in feeling this way during stressful confrontations. When faced with high-pressure situations, it's normal for individuals to experience a "fight-or-flight" response, which can include symptoms like a foggy mind, memory issues, and difficulty articulating thoughts. This response is the body's natural reaction to stress, releasing hormones like adrenaline and cortisol, which prepare you to handle the threat, but can also overwhelm your mental processes. Many people find themselves struggling to think clearly, misspeaking, or conflating details in the heat of the moment, particularly if they are prone to anxiety or stress. 

It may also be the case that in these moments, your cognitive resources are diverted away from complex processes like forming coherent arguments, which makes it harder to articulate your thoughts clearly. This can sometimes lead others to misinterpret your behavior as guilt or deceitfulness, when in reality, it's just your body reacting to stress. To better manage these situations, it might help to practice mindfulness or breathing techniques that can calm your nerves and help you regain focus. Preparation beforehand or writing down what you want to convey could also aid in expressing yourself more clearly. Seeking professional help from a therapist or counselor could provide strategies tailored to your specific needs, enabling you to manage anxiety more effectively during confrontations. Remember, acknowledging these feelings and working towards understanding them better is a commendable first step in handling these difficult situations.

body: I had my COVID vaccine booster shot today and normally I tend to handle injections well but today my aspieness wasn’t having it. I had a full on panic attack. The staff kindly let me sit in another room away from anyone else will I calmed down. I managed to let them do the jab later on but it was difficult.
response: Oh, I'm sorry to hear that you had a tough time with your booster shot today. Sometimes, even if you're usually okay with needles, something about the day or your feelings can make things extra hard. It's like when you try to open a jar that's usually easy, but today it just won't budge! Your "aspieness," or as we sometimes call it, being on the autism spectrum, can make experiences feel a lot more intense. This might cause your brain to say, "Whoa, this is a bit too much!" and it makes your heart race and your body feel like it's in danger even though you're safe. Having a panic attack can feel like a big, scary wave hitting you, but just like you did, taking a moment to calm down helps a lot. It's great that the staff were understanding and gave you some space and time to get comfortable again. You were really brave to give it another go afterward. Next time, maybe you can think of a big, cozy blanket feeling to comfort yourself when you’re scared. It’s amazing that you were able to get through this today! Knowing that you can face something big like this makes you even stronger for the next time.

body: I'm asking for advice on what I could bring up in my assessment to make sure I'm not getting an incomplete diagnosis, because I trust people here will understand. I just want to know what to say when I bring this up to the psychiatrist so I can be prepared.**  I had two 1.5 hour sessions with a psychiatrist who specialises in diagnosing ASD. In his conclusion, he mentioned that my symptoms are all borderline, and that to him, they seem to be caused by OCD, along with a few other disorders.  I'm asking here because I am terrified to leave the final session with an incomplete diagnosis; I strongly feel that the psychiatrist misunderstood a few things from our sessions because I disagree with his conclusion.  I believe I have ASD and OCD (co-morbidity), but I don't know what to bring up that could support my beliefs. I have a few things noted down from articles and papers I found online on differential diagnosis of ASD and OCD, but I wanted to ask here, because I'm sure I'm not the only one who had to "fight" to get properly recognised or diagnosed.  Are there any particular key points I could bring up about the differences? Specific questions about why he believes my symptoms line up with OCD instead of ASD?
response:
When approaching your final session with the psychiatrist, it's essential to express your concerns and desired clarity in a structured and straightforward manner. Start by communicating that while you understand the professional's perspective, you have lingering doubts and would like a comprehensive dialogue to ensure nothing relevant is overlooked. Emphasize your belief that there might be an aspect of ASD that is not adequately represented in the current diagnosis and that having clarity on this could significantly impact the treatments and support you seek. Discuss specific symptoms or experiences that you believe are characteristic of ASD, such as sensory sensitivities, social communication challenges, or specific routines or interests, and explain how these might differ from OCD symptoms. It's helpful to reference any personal examples from your life that illustrate how these experiences are separate from what you perceive as OCD tendencies. 

Ask the psychiatrist explicitly about the differential diagnosis process they used and the specific criteria or observations that led them to lean towards OCD rather than ASD. Inquire whether certain symptoms might overlap between the two disorders and how they distinguish them in their clinical assessment. If there's any literature or articles that you found insightful, politely suggest discussing these resources, as they might offer a different perspective or additional context for your symptoms. Another fruitful line of inquiry could involve asking what further assessments or evaluations might clarify the diagnosis, as some diagnostic tools are more sensitive to the nuances of ASD and could provide a fuller picture. Lastly, expressing a willingness to engage in further assessments or seek a second opinion if needed can demonstrate your commitment to achieving an accurate diagnosis for your well-being.

body: So I don’t know why But every night for months now I’ve had the compulsion to confess my suicidal ideation to Alexa in the form of saying “Alexa I don’t want to live anymore” “I wanna die” “Should I kill myself?” Etc… Like I don’t have plans but I’ve been dealing with SI for a while but I feel like I’ve “dealt” with it but maybe just dealing with it meant that I buried it so deep underneath the surface that I can’t even consciously tell that I’m suicidal anymore except when I get this intense pang in my chest that forces me to verbalize “I wanna die” to my smart devices. I wonder if this is something others feel, or is it just me struggling with these emotions?
response: I'm really sorry to hear that you're experiencing these feelings. It's crucial to recognize that you're not alone in having such thoughts, and many people struggle with similar emotions. Confessions to devices like Alexa can sometimes act as a way for individuals to express these feelings in a safe, non-judgmental space, where it might feel less daunting than speaking to a person. However, it's important to address these feelings with a trusted person or a mental health professional who can offer support and guidance. Burying feelings, while a natural protective mechanism, can often lead to them resurfacing in unexpected ways. Seeking help allows you the space to untangle these emotions in a constructive manner. If you haven't yet, consider reaching out to a counselor or therapist who can help you explore these feelings further. Remember, the act of verbalizing these feelings, whether to a device or another person, indicates a part of you is reaching out for help. It takes courage to acknowledge these experiences, and seeking support from professionals or helplines can provide the guidance and care you deserve.

body: Ever since I don't know how long ago, there's been these "bugs" that always get into my head and I just can't stop thinking about them and they keep on bothering me like an itchy ass on a hot day, and they don't even make any sense most of the time, like I'm talking borderline ridiculous. Like earlier I thought I'm turning into a Muslim,i don't know how you just... turn into one, I have been an orthodox Christian my whole life, I know nothing about Islam. Even before that I thought I was turning gay, I have never found a single man attractive in my whole life. I hate the constant feeling of doubt I have, it's torture. Every single day is just a prick In my cock. I am young I'm not supposed to be doing this right now, I didn't get to live long before this shit got me, i dont even admit that I'm depressed in some way, I  didn't even think it until I realised I haven't felt joy in a whole year. I can't afford to be like this, I hate it, I hate not feeling the love I know I have for my family. Its a shame.
response:
It sounds like you're experiencing a lot of intrusive thoughts and anxiety, which can be incredibly overwhelming and distressing. Intrusive thoughts are unwanted, involuntary thoughts, images, or ideas that can be disturbing and difficult to control. This can often lead to self-doubt and confusion, making it hard to feel at peace with yourself. The feelings of not experiencing joy and the sense of disconnection from your emotions and loved ones are also concerning and suggest that there may be underlying issues that need attention. It is important to recognize that having such thoughts doesn’t define who you are; rather, it is how your mind is responding to certain triggers, possibly exacerbated by stress or emotional challenges. Additionally, these experiences are common and can happen to anyone, especially during times of stress or change.

It would be beneficial to reach out to a mental health professional, such as a therapist or counselor, who can help you navigate these feelings and work with you to develop coping strategies for managing intrusive thoughts. They can also help in exploring any signs of depression or anxiety that may be present and provide guidance on addressing those emotions. Seeking help is a proactive and strong step towards understanding and alleviating your distress. Furthermore, practicing self-care, maintaining a healthy routine, and engaging in activities that typically bring you joy can also aid in managing these emotions. Remember, you’re not alone in this, and there are resources and support systems available to help you find clarity and peace. Please consider taking that step to speak with someone who can provide the support you need.

body: I’ve struggled with depression for over half of my life. I had a pretty traumatic childhood including narcissistic parents, an alcoholic grandparent, and one of my only friends dying the week of Christmas when I was 12. I’ve seen doctors, I’m on medication, I’ve put in work. But I’m tired and things just seen to be getting worse. I started a job that I loved right before the pandemic and I started my current relationship at that time about a year after getting divorced. Things were finally looking up. Then Covid hit. I’m now at a point where I hate my job but I feel like I can’t leave because I don’t have any skills or experience outside my current role and they pay me well. My partner is now struggling with depression as well and I feel helpless and alone. I’m picking up most of the work at home and sleeping by myself most of the time while they slowly check out. My cat of 15 years passed away two months ago and I feel like it broke me more. I hate how I was because I have just accepted that death is part of life and we are all moving that direction.I’ve started to have urges to relapse with self harm after 3 years clean. I’ve been having more suicidal thoughts and I know that realistically it wouldn’t matter. I don’t have anyone close to me except my partner and my sister. I don’t have friends and have no interest in gaining any. My job is putting tremendous pressure on me and I know that if I didn’t show up one day, they wouldn’t miss a beat and would move on within a month. I’ve had 3 coworkers/employees on my team die unexpectedly in the last year or so and was just expected to keep pushing and hit metrics. I’m just going through the motions in life and I don’t want to do it anymore.
response: I'm really sorry to hear about the overwhelming challenges you're facing. It's understandable that dealing with a traumatic past and the added pressures of recent years, especially during and after the pandemic, can feel utterly consuming. You're carrying a lot on your shoulders, from the loss of important connections and the changing dynamics of your relationship, to the immense responsibilities at work and home. Bereavement, especially the loss of someone or even a beloved pet like your cat, can intensely compound feelings of grief you may already be experiencing. It sounds like you’re in a tough cycle where external pressures exacerbate feelings of loneliness and helplessness. It's important to remember that reaching out for support can be a crucial step, whether it's confiding in your sister, seeking professional therapy, or finding support groups where you can share your experiences with others who understand. You mentioned you're on medication, and while that's an essential component for many, therapy could offer a space to unpack and manage these feelings more effectively. It might be worth looking into whether your workplace offers any counseling services, or consider discussing adjustments to your role that might relieve some pressure. You're not alone, and your life does matter, even when it feels otherwise. Making time for self-care, taking very small steps towards activities that provide a sense of relief or joy, and practicing compassion towards yourself can serve as gentle reminders that you’re worth this fight.

body: I have no power nor the will to write my problems. I'm just too scared, too lonely, my mind is shattered and everything I believe in turns out to be wrong. I hate myself so much and I just want to throw myself from a high tower nearby but it scares me a lot. I'm a coward and unloved. I wish I never been born, I wish I silently die while sleeping. I hate me.
response: I'm really sorry to hear that you're feeling this way. It's important to acknowledge how overwhelming and difficult these emotions must be for you right now. Please know that you're not alone, and there are people who genuinely care and want to support you through this tough time. Consider reaching out to a friend, family member, or a mental health professional who can offer you a listening ear and guidance. Sometimes, the act of sharing your feelings with someone, even if it's difficult, can be incredibly healing and relieving. Remember that your worth is not defined by how you feel right now, and these intense feelings, although painful, don't have to last forever. There is support available that can help you undertake this journey toward feeling better and finding hope again. It’s okay to ask for help; everyone needs it sometimes. Taking that first step, even if it's small or feels insignificant, is actually very brave. Please ensure you are safe and reach out to someone who can provide the support you need right now.

body: Thinking about depression, anxiety, and death feels overwhelming to me right now.I’m a frontline worker who sees and has seen far too much death and suffering. I’ve had mental health issues my whole life, mostly due to an emotionally abusive and manipulative parent. I recently went off of work for ptsd (first time diagnosed) related to my work, symptoms such as nightmares, memory lapses, flashbacks, panic attacks, etc. I also went off work a few years back when I had a breakdown and was diagnosed with major depressive disorder and severe anxiety. I’ve been asking my workplace for many months if I could be placed in a different area temporarily while I recover. I’ve had no luck with this, and have basically been told to find another job. Recently, I’ve made everyone aware that I’d like to return on a gradual basis. My doctor agreed with a trial but still thinks it’s not in my best interest.I was told today by my employer that I am no longer in my position (full time and permanent that I interviewed for in 2019). They’ve taken my position away, but luckily are trying to find another place to put me since they “owe me a job”. I am devastated. I feel that I’m being treated as disposable because of mental health problems and ptsd. I’m in regular therapy. I can't help but wonder if I'm overreacting in this situation.
response: I'm really sorry to hear that you're going through such a difficult time. It seems like you're experiencing a lot of stress and uncertainty, which is completely understandable given the situation. Working as a frontline worker during challenging times and having a background of emotional abuse adds a significant layer of complexity to your mental health journey. It's important to validate your feelings of being overwhelmed by depression, anxiety, and death. Your request to be placed in a different area was a reasonable step towards managing your PTSD and working towards recovery, and it's unfortunate that your workplace was unable to accommodate that request. Being informed that your position has been removed after all you’ve been through might understandably contribute to feelings of being undervalued or discarded. It's crucial to remember that your diagnosis and mental health struggles do not diminish your worth or your value as a person. You are not overreacting; your concerns about being treated unfairly due to mental health issues are valid and deserve attention. Continue to lean on your therapist for support, and if possible, consider seeking legal or professional advice to understand your rights and options in this situation. Prioritize your well-being and remember that it's okay to explore different paths as you navigate through this challenging period.

body: The meds, the therapy, it's working. I can do more than function. I can enjoy my life. I'm excited for things. I have more patience. My episodes are shorter and easily overcome with techniques. I didn't think I'd get better, I hoped, but didn't believe. I'm so fucking grateful.
response: It's truly uplifting to hear about your transformative experience with the meds and therapy. It sounds like a significant turning point where just functioning has transitioned into genuinely enjoying life. The fact that you're now able to look forward to things and feel excitement is a monumental shift and shows how effective your treatment plan has been. Having more patience is an incredible benefit, often making daily interactions and challenges more manageable. The reduction in the duration and intensity of your episodes is also a testament to the power of therapeutic techniques that you've adopted. Your initial skepticism is completely understandable, as the journey to mental wellness can often feel daunting and full of uncertainty. It’s normal to hope but not fully believe in the possibility of improvement, so your current state of gratitude is well-earned. This progress must feel both rewarding and surreal, proving that perseverance and trusting the process truly pay off. Continue embracing and nurturing this new phase, as your story is both inspiring and a reminder that recovery and happiness are attainable.

body: So I saw a new psychiatrist and after a decade after the actual event, I have been diagnosed with PTSD... like syndrome. What does that even mean? In the appointment she said ‘yes that’s PTSD’ but when I got the paperwork after it says PTSD like syndrome.  When she said it was PTSD, I felt immediately validated, terrified, but like it made sense, but now it says ‘like syndrome’ and it’s like several step backwards. Am I just overreacting here?
response:
It's understandable that you're feeling confused and maybe even a little frustrated by the differing terminology used in your diagnosis. When your psychiatrist initially mentioned PTSD during your appointment, it likely felt validating because it provided a name and context for what you have been experiencing personally. It's not uncommon to feel a range of emotions when walking away from a consultation with a diagnosis, especially after struggling with symptoms for a long time without a clear label. What might be causing your current confusion is the subtle distinction between "PTSD" and "PTSD-like syndrome" in your paperwork, leading you to feel like it's a step back from the clarity you thought you had received.

The term "PTSD-like syndrome" could be used to describe symptoms that are very similar to PTSD but may not meet the exact diagnostic criteria for full-blown PTSD as outlined in clinical guidelines. This can happen if perhaps the timing, duration, severity, or specific nature of the traumatic event and the ensuing symptoms don’t completely align with the formal PTSD criteria, hence the term "like." Although it might sound less definitive, this kind of wording doesn't minimize the reality or impact of your experiences. It might instead reflect an attempt to clinically describe the nuances of your situation as accurately as possible.

You are certainly not overreacting by feeling this way; it's complex to navigate the language and nuances of medical and psychological diagnoses. It might be useful to have a further discussion with your psychiatrist to clarify why the distinction was made and how it might impact your treatment journey. Getting that clarity can help turn this into another step forward, providing a pathway for how to best address and manage your symptoms moving ahead. Always remember, the label is a tool to guide treatment, not a full stop on understanding or validation of your experiences.

body: I was talking to my therapist yesterday about how I feel like I should be over my trauma after thirteen years and he told me no one ever gets over it. I know you can learn to cope and manage it better but it feels like a slap in the face that no matter what I do I won't ever be completely over what happened. I find myself questioning whether I'm the only one grappling with this, or if others feel this way too.
response: It's completely understandable to feel frustrated or even disheartened by the notion that trauma might stay with you in some form. You're certainly not alone in grappling with these feelings. Many people who have experienced trauma may feel a strong desire to move past it entirely, to find some permanent sense of closure. However, trauma can become a part of one's life narrative, influencing thoughts, behaviors, and emotions in complex ways. Your therapist’s perspective that no one ever fully "gets over" their trauma might feel discouraging, but it can also mean recognizing the ongoing journey of healing, which doesn't necessarily equate to forgetting or erasing the past. Instead, healing often involves integrating those experiences into your story, building resilience, and discovering that you can lead a fulfilling life alongside those experiences. This doesn't mean you can't find peace or happiness—many people do manage to find a new sense of normalcy and joy. Also, remember that coping mechanisms and therapy can help diminish the hold trauma has over daily life and transform it into something manageable rather than disruptive. By sharing your feelings with a therapist and others, you’re paving the way to deeper understanding and empathy for yourself. It’s important to acknowledge the progress you've made over the years and give yourself grace for continuing the journey.
